wikidata_org_candidates.label: Stromberg-Carlson
wikidata_org_candidates.description: telecommunications equipment and electronics manufacturing company in the United States
wikidata_org_candidates.reasoning: The name is an exact match. Stromberg-Carlson was a well-known electronics and telecommunications company with a significant facility in San Diego (as noted on the card). Specifically, their San Diego division was famous for developing the SC-4020 digital film recorder, which aligns perfectly with the specialized skills listed on the card such as 'Digital film recorders', 'computer animation', and 'graphics'.

results.possibleIdentity: William C. Dwyer was a computer programmer and graphics specialist active during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He was employed by the Stromberg Carlson Corporation (specifically its Data Products Division, later known as DatagraphiX) in San Diego, California. Dwyer specialized in the field of digital computers, software design, and computer animation, particularly in relation to digital film recorders such as the S-C 4020 and S-C 4060. In 1968, while associated with the Polytechnic Institute of Brooklyn, he presented a technical paper titled "WINDOWS, SHIELDS, AND SHADING" at the annual conference of UAIDE (Users of Automatic Information Display Equipment), the primary user group for Stromberg-Carlson equipment. His expertise in computer graphics and film recording led to his association with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit organization that brokered collaborations between technical professionals and artists. Dwyer likely provided technical support and programming assistance to artists utilizing Stromberg-Carlson's microfilm and film recording technology for early computer-generated art and animation.
